narrative_summary:
The MSH2 p.Met1Leu (Met1Leu) substitution alters the initiator methionine of the DNA mismatch‑repair protein MSH2 and has been reported in the context of Lynch syndrome–associated malignancies such as colorectal, ovarian and sebaceous carcinomas. 
Clinical reports provide divergent interpretations. In several cohorts the variant was classified as pathogenic, for example in a colorectal‑cancer screening series that identified it as a disease‑causing mutation [PMC8864140], in an ovarian‑cancer population study that listed it among rare pathogenic MSH2 alterations [PMC3493867], and in a recent analysis of patients who did not meet Amsterdam or Bethesda criteria, where it was highlighted as a pathogenic LS‑associated change [PMC10589993]. Conversely, other investigations have recorded the same change as a variant of uncertain significance (VUS) or have downgraded its status: a re‑classification study noted an initial likely‑pathogenic label that was later revised to VUS [PMC6355179]; a Nigerian prostate‑tumor exome dataset annotated it as VUS [PMC10010347]; and a pediatric therapy‑related myeloid neoplasm cohort also reported it as VUS based on ACMG criteria [PMC7880998]. 
Functional assays consistently suggest a lack of deleterious effect. A massively parallel functional test of MSH2 missense variants classified p.Met1Leu as functionally neutral/benign [PMC7820803]. No experimental data indicating loss of protein function or mismatch‑repair deficiency were presented in the other studies. 
Computational predictions and population observations further support a benign interpretation. In silico assessments described the variant as neutral or predicted to have no impact on protein function in multiple supplementary tables [PMC7820803; PMC9325712]. The allele is extremely rare in population databases, with only a few reported carriers and no homozygotes, consistent with a low‑frequency germline change [PMC10010347]. 
Overall, the evidence for MSH2 p.Met1Leu is conflicted. While some clinical series have labeled it pathogenic, the preponderance of functional data, computational predictions, and its rarity without clear segregation evidence point toward a likely benign or uncertain significance. At present the variant cannot be definitively classified as pathogenic, and further segregation or functional studies are needed to resolve its clinical relevance.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study classifies sebaceous carcinomas into three distinct mutational subclasses—UV‑damage, microsatellite‑instability, and ocular—linking mutation patterns to tumor origin and clinical outcome.
article_mentions.title: Cell of origin and mutation pattern define three clinically distinct classes of sebaceous carcinoma

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that LS patients lacking Amsterdam or Bethesda criteria can harbor pathogenic MMR gene variants such as MSH2 p.Met1Leu, underscoring underdiagnosis and supporting universal screening approaches.
article_mentions.title: Diagnosis of patients with Lynch syndrome lacking the Amsterdam II or Bethesda criteria

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study compares genetic mutation patterns between complete and non‑complete responders to neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy in locally advanced rectal cancer, highlighting several genes with differing mutation frequencies but providing no specific functional or clinical interpretation for the MSH2 p.Met1Leu variant.
article_mentions.title: Discriminating Potential Genetic Markers for Complete Response and Non-Complete Response Patients to Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy with Locally Advanced Rectal Cancer

article_mentions.title: The effects of genomic germline variant reclassification on clinical cancer care

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study compares screening methods for Lynch syndrome in ovarian cancer and concludes that sequential IHC with MLH1 promoter methylation followed by MSI offers the highest sensitivity and specificity.
article_mentions.title: Performance characteristics of screening strategies to identify Lynch syndrome in women with ovarian cancer
